reply.proposal: Audit the current hand-rolled middleware in packages/api to document implicit dependencies and define a canonical order: request-id -> logging -> rate-limiting -> auth -> validation. Replace the custom chaining with Hono's typed onion-model middleware system, which enforces consistent ordering via app.use() and provides a typed context. Re-implement each concern as an isolated, unit-tested middleware using battle-tested libraries: pino-http with Pino for structured logging, jose for JWT auth verification, and @upstash/ratelimit backed by Redis for distributed rate limiting. Centralize all registration in a createApp() factory that builds the middleware pipeline in the fixed order and injects Zod validation via @hono/zod-validator for type-safe requests. Add contract/integration tests that assert ordering, error propagation, and context values to prevent future drift.

Install @reduxjs/toolkit and react-redux in apps/dashboard and scaffold a centralized store at src/app/store.ts using configureStore with DevTools enabled. Create domain slices under src/features/ (e.g., authSlice, dashboardSlice, uiSlice) using createSlice with TypeScript interfaces to replace scattered useState/Context logic. Add typed helpers useAppDispatch and useAppSelector in src/app/hooks.ts and wrap the dashboard root layout (_app.tsx / layout.tsx) with <Provider store={store}> as a Client Component. Adopt RTK Query inside the store for API fetching/caching to further reduce local state complexity. Migrate incrementally starting with auth and global UI state, keeping existing logic working until each feature is cut over.

Add prisma as devDependency and @prisma/client as dependency in packages/api, then run npx prisma init to scaffold prisma/schema.prisma and .env with DATABASE_URL. Configure the datasource provider (e.g., postgresql) and generator client in schema.prisma, and port existing table definitions into initial Prisma models. Create a singleton PrismaClient wrapper at src/lib/prisma.ts using a globalThis guard to prevent multiple instances in dev, and export it for all API handlers. Add npm scripts for prisma generate, migrate dev, and studio, run the first migration, and replace manual SQL strings with the typed client, giving us full type-safe db access.

Create a dedicated, injectable BillingProviderClient class under packages/api/src/integrations/billing/ that encapsulates all REST calls and is configured via typed env config. Use Node's native fetch (undici) with AbortSignal.timeout for per-request timeouts (e.g. 5s connect + 5s total) and wrap it with p-retry using exponential backoff with jitter for retryable statuses (429, 502/503/504) and network errors, respecting Retry-After and sending an Idempotency-Key for safe retries. Validate all request/response payloads with Zod schemas and normalize failures into a typed BillingError hierarchy (TimeoutError, RetryExhaustedError, ProviderError) with statusCode, correlationId, and sanitized body. Instrument with Pino structured logging and OpenTelemetry tracing/metrics for latency, retry count, and error rate to enable alerts and good error reporting.

Keep the long-lived session token in a HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax or Strict cookie so it is automatically sent by the browser and cannot be stolen via XSS. For SPA fetch calls, use fetch with credentials: 'include' instead of having JavaScript read and attach the token. If a manual Authorization header is required, use a short-lived (5-15 min) access JWT kept only in memory (module variable/closure, never persisted) with a long-lived refresh token in HttpOnly cookie using rotation. Protect the cookie flow with SameSite plus anti-CSRF token/custom header validation and a strict Content-Security-Policy to mitigate XSS.
acknowledged_rejections: Non-HttpOnly JavaScript-readable cookie for session token
Embedding session token in URL query parameters

reply.proposal: Install jest, ts-jest and @types/jest as devDependencies in packages/api to enable TypeScript-native testing. Create packages/api/jest.config.js with preset set to ts-jest, testEnvironment set to node, roots pointing to src directory and testMatch set to co-located *.test.ts files for handler unit tests. Add test scripts test, test:watch and test:coverage to packages/api/package.json and add a lightweight jest.setup.ts to mock shared concerns like auth and database clients. Validate the setup with a sample unit test for an existing handler that mocks request and response objects and asserts status and payload.

For the marketing pages in apps/dashboard we will use Next.js built-in CSS Modules with co-located styles. Each component will live in its own folder with its stylesheet next to it, e.g., Hero/Hero.tsx and Hero/Hero.module.css. Shared design tokens for colors, spacing, and typography will be defined as CSS Variables in a single tokens.css file imported at the marketing layout root. This satisfies the designer's preference for component-scoped styles that live with the component, with zero runtime overhead and no extra tooling.

Consolidate the database model into a single Prisma schema file (schema.prisma) as the canonical source of truth, removing manual SQL DDL from the workflow. Configure Prisma Migrate with the database provider and a shadow database for diffing. Replace hand-writing SQL by running `npx prisma migrate dev` which diffs the current schema.prisma against the migration history and auto-generates versioned SQL files in prisma/migrations. Commit those generated files and apply them in staging and production via `npx prisma migrate deploy` as part of the CI/CD pipeline. Add a CI check that fails if schema.prisma has changed without a corresponding generated migration to keep schema and migrations in sync.

Introduce a GraphQL gateway layer in front of our existing domain services. Define a unified typed schema for current resources with resolvers that delegate to the existing REST/service layer. Mobile clients will request only the fields needed per screen via GraphQL selection sets, eliminating overfetching. Enforce query complexity limits, depth limiting, and persisted queries to protect backend performance. Deprecate the overfetching REST endpoints gradually as screens migrate to the new endpoint.

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Audit existing hand-rolled middleware in packages/api and consolidate into idiomatic Hono middleware composed in a single explicit order using app.use() and createMiddleware factories. Standardize the chain as: pino request logger (child logger with requestId for one JSON line per request) -> OpenTelemetry trace init (10% sampled) -> token-bucket rate limiting per API key (tier config looked up via postgres.js) -> auth that reads httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookie and validates against the Postgres UNLOGGED session cache -> Zod validation at the boundary. Move each middleware to packages/api/src/middleware/* with typed Hono Variables/AppEnv, all errors funneled through the existing problem+json error handler. Add node:test integration tests asserting ordering (401/429 short-circuit before handlers) and document the canonical order in packages/api README to prevent drift.
acknowledged_rejections: Migrating API server from Hono to Express/Fastify/Koa/Nest
Switching REST with OpenAPI to GraphQL or tRPC
Replacing Postgres UNLOGGED session/rate-limit storage with Redis

reply.proposal: Keep Zustand for apps/dashboard global state per our prior decision - the surface is still small and Redux adds unnecessary boilerplate and bundle size. Refactor the growing store into typed domain slices (session, filters, toasts, etc.) under apps/dashboard/src/stores/ using Zustand's slice pattern, combined into a single store. Document store boundaries and update the dashboard README with run/test notes for the new structure.

reply.proposal: We will not introduce Prisma and will continue using postgres.js with hand-written SQL and typed row mappers living next to each query as decided. This preserves visibility into query plans, keeps us on a single abstraction-free runtime layer, and aligns with our forward-only numbered SQL migrations that are reviewed as literal SQL. To address the type-safety concern without an ORM, we will tighten our existing pattern by co-locating TypeScript row types with each query and enforcing them with our existing Zod schemas at the API boundary, adding compile-time SQL validation via postgres.js typing if needed. This avoids adding a second migration system and a heavy client runtime that would conflict with our UNLOGGED session table benchmarking and minimal-dependency goals.
acknowledged_rejections: Prisma ORM and Prisma Client in packages/api
Prisma Migrate replacing hand-written SQL migrations
Replacing SQL strings with ORM abstraction for type-safe access

reply.proposal: Extend the existing native fetch retry wrapper in packages/api into a dedicated billingProvider client module with base URL, API key, and default timeout read from environment variables. Implement per-request timeouts via AbortController / AbortSignal.timeout and retry with exponential backoff + jitter only on retryable 429/5xx and network errors, capped at 3 attempts. Validate all provider responses with Zod at the boundary and translate failures to our problem+json envelope, logging request_id, status, latency, and attempt count with pino as a single JSON line. Add an OpenTelemetry span around each outbound call with attributes for provider route and attempt, respecting the existing 10% sampling.

reply.proposal: Keep session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies as previously decided and let the browser attach them automatically. The SPA should use our native fetch wrapper with credentials: 'include' and never access the raw token in JavaScript. This preserves XSS resistance - a script injection cannot exfiltrate httpOnly cookies - while reusing the existing Hono auth middleware and Postgres UNLOGGED session cache. If the mobile web client needs cross-origin calls, tighten CORS allowlist and use Secure cookies rather than exposing the token to JS.
key_technologies: native fetch with credentials:include and retry wrapper
Postgres UNLOGGED table for session cache
acknowledged_rejections: Storing session token in localStorage / sessionStorage / JavaScript-readable cookie and manually attaching via Authorization header
Keeping raw token in Zustand client state or in-memory store for SPA to send

reply.proposal: Keep packages/api on the established node:test runner instead of introducing Jest. Refactor request handlers to accept injected dependencies (postgres.js client, config) and test them via Hono's in-process fetch helper using node:test and node:assert/strict, without starting a real HTTP server. Add an npm test script that builds TypeScript and runs `node --test` so CI reuses the existing reusable workflow with no transform config. Update packages/api README with a minimal handler test example to unblock the team. This preserves zero test-framework dependencies and fast CI.

reply.proposal: We should not adopt auto-generated migrations — our agreed approach is hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations reviewed as literal SQL, paired with postgres.js and hand-written queries so query plans stay visible. Instead of codegen, improve the existing flow with a CI job that applies migrations to an ephemeral Postgres (via docker compose) and dumps docs/schema.sql for review, plus a lint that migrations are forward-only and numbered. This keeps the stack at one datastore, avoids an ORM/schema DSL, and preserves linear history for prod and CI. Type mismatches are still caught by typed row mappers next to queries and Zod at the API boundary.

reply.proposal: Keep the public API as REST on Hono at /v1 per the prior decision and solve overfetching with sparse fieldsets as already agreed. Project decision log:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
  - REJECTED: Express — Larger dependency surface and slower cold starts; the middleware ecosystem is tempting but everything we need exists for Hono. Migration was evaluated and declined 2026-03

- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
  - REJECTED: Redux Toolkit — Boilerplate and indirection outweigh benefits at our state size; the team spent more time on slices than features in the spike

- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
  - REJECTED: Prisma — Query engine adds cold-start latency on serverless deploys and hides generated SQL; we hit both in the 2026-02 spike
  - REJECTED: TypeORM — Decorator-based entities fought our functional codebase style
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
  - REJECTED: axios — Interceptor chains obscured a double-retry bug in 2026-01; native fetch plus our wrapper is smaller and easier to audit
  - REJECTED: request — Deprecated upstream since 2020; unpatched vulnerabilities

Enforce a single documented order via a shared createApiApp() builder: pino request logger -> token-bucket rate limiter per API key (backed by the existing Postgres UNLOGGED table) -> auth (httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookie validation) -> Zod boundary validation. Add node:test coverage for each middleware in isolation plus an integration test asserting correct ordering and early-exit behavior.

reply.proposal: Do not introduce Prisma in packages/api — it was explicitly rejected in the 2026-02 spike because the query engine adds cold-start latency on serverless and hides generated SQL from review. The current decision is postgres.js with hand-written SQL and typed row mappers collocated with each query to keep plans visible. To get stronger type safety without an ORM, enhance the existing pattern by generating TypeScript row types from the hand-written forward-only migrations and enforcing them via the postgres.js typed query helper and the existing Zod boundary schemas. This satisfies the type-safety goal while preserving one datastore, visible SQL, and the prior architectural decision.

reply.proposal: Reuse the existing native fetch with small retry wrapper in packages/api for the billing provider integration, configuring it with AbortController timeouts and exponential backoff with jitter for retries. Wrap calls with Zod validation for request and response payloads to keep boundary validation consistent and improve error reporting. Log request attempts, retries and final failures with pino structured logs including trace IDs and map provider errors into our standard problem+json envelope. This avoids new dependencies, preserves auditability of outbound HTTP, and aligns with the one-wrapper pattern already used elsewhere.

reply.proposal: Retain httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax cookies for session tokens; never expose the raw token to JavaScript. The SPA sends requests using native fetch with credentials: 'same-origin' (or 'include' for cross-site) so the browser attaches the cookie automatically. This preserves the XSS mitigation validated in the 2026-04 pentest where JS-readable storage was exfiltrated. If mobile web needs explicit header attachment, use a separate non-sensitive CSRF token or BFF endpoint rather than making the session token readable.

reply.proposal: Keep REST and implement sparse fieldsets via `?fields=id,name,created_at` as agreed when we decided to stay REST to cover overfetching. Add an optional `fields` query param to each resource endpoint, validated by a per-resource Zod enum schema against an allowlist, defaulting to the full representation when omitted for backwards compatibility and HTTP cache-friendliness. In the postgres.js query layer, translate the validated list into an explicit SELECT column list with typed row mappers so we avoid overfetching at the DB, wire, and client layers. Document the param and allowed values in the OpenAPI spec, include it in cache keys and OpenTelemetry trace attributes, and emit pino structured logs for field usage to guide mobile team adoption.

This keeps REST, Hono portability, and security properties while fixing ergonomics.

This keeps the dependency footprint at zero, matches our existing outbound HTTP pattern, and is easy to unit test with fetch mocks.

  - REJECTED: MobX — Implicit reactivity made renders hard to trace during the spike
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier

Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
  - REJECTED: auto-generated migrations — A generated diff silently included a column drop that reached staging in 2026-03 — rolled back; generated DDL is no longer trusted for schema changes
  - REJECTED: down migrations — False confidence — most destructive changes cannot actually be reversed; restore-from-backup is the real rollback
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
  - REJECTED: Prisma — Query engine adds cold-start latency on serverless deploys and hides generated SQL; we hit both in the 2026-02 spike
  - REJECTED: TypeORM — Decorator-based entities fought our functional codebase style
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab

reply.proposal: We should not move to auto-generated migrations. Per our March 2026 rollback, generated DDL is not trusted to produce safe schema changes and every migration must be reviewed as literal SQL. Keep the existing hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migration pattern. To address the pain behind this task, add a CI check that applies all hand-written migrations to an ephemeral Postgres and diffs the resulting schema against the canonical schema definition file - fail CI on drift. This gives us a safety net without ever executing unreviewed generated DDL.
key_technologies: ephemeral Postgres in CI for schema drift check

reply.proposal: Keep the public API as REST with URL path versioning (/v1) and OpenAPI spec, and solve overfetching with sparse fieldsets. Clients can request exactly what they need via `?fields=id,name,avatar_url` or JSON:API style `?fields[users]=id,name` for compound documents. The server validates the requested fields against an allowlist per resource defined in OpenAPI and projects both the DB select and JSON serialization. This preserves HTTP caching (query string is part of cache key), works for all language clients, and directly addresses the mobile overfetching without adding a new query language.
key_technologies: Sparse fieldsets via fields query parameter
